title: Karl Marx
summary: The German philosopher, economist, and revolutionary whose analysis of capitalism as a system of class exploitation — developed in Capital, the Communist Manifesto, and decades of political journalism — became the theoretical foundation of socialist and communist movements worldwide, inspired revolutions that shaped the 20th century, and remains the most systematic critique of capitalism ever produced.
hook: Marx predicted that capitalism would produce its own gravediggers — that the working class, immiserated by industrial capitalism, would eventually overthrow it. He was wrong about the timing, wrong about the mechanism, and wrong about what would come after. But his analysis of how capitalism works — the labor theory of value, surplus value, commodity fetishism, the tendency toward concentration — remains the most rigorous critique of market economies ever produced. Understanding the modern world without Marx is like navigating without a map that's partly wrong: still the most useful thing available.
indexTerms: tendency of the rate of profit to fall

plainEnglish.theFigure:
At one point, governments that used his name ruled over a third of all people on Earth. Karl Marx died in 1883 in a rented flat in London. He had little idea this would happen. He spent his days reading in the British Museum. He wrote dense books that very few people bought. He borrowed money from his friend Friedrich Engels to pay rent. He was a philosopher. He had been thrown out of three countries for his radical ideas. He ran no government. He led no army. But the ideas he wrote down in cold reading rooms changed the 20th century more than almost any general or king.

Marx grew up in Germany in the early 1800s. At that time, factories were replacing farms. Workers were flooding into cities. He watched factory owners grow rich while workers stayed poor. He wanted to know why. That question drove everything he wrote. He spent decades trying to answer it.
plainEnglish.worldOfIdeas:
Marx started with a simple question: where does profit come from? He had a clear answer. Workers make things. Everything they make has value because of the work they put in. But workers do not get paid the full value of what they make. The factory owner keeps the difference. Marx called this extra amount surplus value. It is the gap between what a worker produces and what the worker is paid. That gap is where profit comes from.

Marx also had a big idea about history. He said history moves because of conflicts between groups with different economic interests. In his time, the main conflict was between factory owners and workers. Rich people controlled the factories, the land, and the tools. Workers had only their labor to sell. This clash of interests drove social change, Marx argued. When the conflict grew sharp enough, it produced revolution.
plainEnglish.centralIdea:
Marx's most important book was Capital, published in 1867. It is long, dense, and difficult. But its central point is not hard to grasp. Capitalism, Marx argued, is a system that hides how it works. When you buy a coat, you see a price tag. You do not see the workers who made it. You do not see the hours they spent. You do not see how much less they were paid than the coat sold for. Marx called this trick commodity fetishism. Things seem to have prices all on their own. The human work behind them becomes invisible.

He also argued that capitalism cannot stand still. Factory owners must keep putting their profits back into the business or fall behind their competitors. This drive to expand creates boom-and-bust cycles. Workers get pushed harder. Machines replace jobs. Wages get squeezed. Marx thought this process would eventually produce a crisis big enough to break the system apart.
plainEnglish.howSpread:
Marx's ideas did not spread widely during his lifetime. His books sold poorly. He lived in poverty for most of his adult years. After he died, his friend Engels edited and published the unfinished writings Marx had left behind. That work made Marx's ideas available to a wider audience.

The big shift came after 1917. Vladimir Lenin led a revolution in Russia and used Marx's ideas as its foundation. The Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, Cuba, and other states all claimed to be building the society Marx had described. By the middle of the 20th century, governments that named Marx as their guide ruled over a large share of the world's population.

But those governments were often very different from anything Marx had written about. He imagined workers taking control of their own lives. What appeared instead, in many cases, were powerful states run by small groups at the top. Scholars still debate how much this came from Marx's ideas and how much from the specific conditions of each country.
plainEnglish.contestedInheritance:
Marx predicted that capitalism would eventually collapse and be replaced by a fairer system. That has not happened. Workers in wealthy countries did not get poorer over time. They got wealthier instead. The communist states that used his name often became harsh dictatorships. The Soviet Union collapsed in 1991. These facts have led many people to say Marx was simply wrong.

But others point out that some of his observations have held up well. He noticed that capitalism tends to concentrate wealth at the top. He saw that financial crises happen again and again. He argued that workers and owners have different interests, and that this tension shapes politics and society. After the financial crash of 2008, sales of Capital actually went up in several countries. His diagnosis of how capitalism works has proved harder to dismiss than his predictions about what would replace it.

beginner.theFigure:
At one point, governments claiming his name ruled over a third of the people on Earth. Karl Marx died in 1883 in a rented London flat, with little idea this would happen. He spent his days reading in the British Museum, writing dense books that very few people bought, and borrowing money from his friend Friedrich Engels to pay rent. He was a philosopher who had been thrown out of three countries for his radical ideas. He ran no government. He led no army. But the ideas he wrote down in cold reading rooms changed the 20th century more than almost any general or king.

Marx grew up in Germany in the early 1800s, when factories were replacing farms and workers were flooding into cities. He watched factory owners grow rich while workers stayed poor. He wanted to know why. That question drove everything he wrote. He spent decades trying to answer it.

Marx started with a simple question: where does profit come from? He had a clear answer. Workers make things. Everything they make has value because of the work they put in. But workers do not get paid the full value of what they make. The factory owner keeps the difference. Marx called this extra amount surplus value. Marx called this trick commodity fetishism. Things seem to have prices all on their own. The human work behind them becomes invisible.

He also argued that capitalism cannot stand still. Factory owners must keep reinvesting their profits or fall behind their competitors. This drive to expand creates boom-and-bust cycles. Workers get pushed harder. Machines replace jobs. Wages get squeezed. Marx thought this process would eventually produce a crisis big enough to break the system apart.

Marx's ideas did not spread widely during his lifetime. His books sold poorly. He lived in poverty for most of his adult years. After he died, his friend Engels edited and published the unfinished manuscripts Marx had left behind. That work made Marx's ideas accessible to a wider audience.

The big shift came after 1917. Vladimir Lenin led a revolution in Russia and used Marx's ideas as its foundation. The Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, Cuba, and other states all claimed to be building the society Marx had described. By the mid-20th century, governments citing Marx ruled over a large share of the world's population.

But those governments were often very different from anything Marx had written about. He imagined workers taking control of their own lives. What appeared instead, in many cases, were powerful states run by small groups at the top. Scholars still debate how much this outcome came from Marx's ideas and how much from the specific conditions of each country.

Marx predicted that capitalism would eventually collapse and be replaced by a fairer system. That has not happened. Workers in wealthy countries did not get poorer over time. They got wealthier instead. The communist states that claimed his name often became harsh dictatorships. The Soviet Union collapsed in 1991. These facts have led many people to say Marx was simply wrong.

But others point out that some of his observations have held up well. He noticed that capitalism tends to concentrate wealth at the top. He saw that financial crises happen again and again. He argued that workers and owners have different interests, and that this tension shapes politics and society. After the financial crash of 2008, sales of Capital actually went up in several countries. His diagnosis of how capitalism works has proved harder to dismiss than his predictions about what would replace it.

general.theFigure:
For several decades in the 20th century, governments claiming his name governed roughly a third of humanity. Karl Marx spent his working life in near-poverty in London, dependent on funds from Friedrich Engels, filling notebooks at the British Museum. He had been expelled from Germany, France, and Belgium for his radical politics before settling in England. He died in 1883 with his major project unfinished and his political movements scattered.

The gap between this obscure life and the colossal aftermath is part of what demands explanation. Marx was a 19th-century German thinker who engaged seriously with the classical economists. He rejected Hegel's idealism while keeping his method. What he produced was, at once, a theory of history, a critique of capitalist economics, and a political program. Separating those three projects from each other is not easy, and that difficulty is one reason arguments about what Marx actually meant have continued for well over a century. Movements from European social democracy to Soviet communism to anti-colonial politics all drew on his ideas, often selectively, and often in ways that clashed with each other.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Marx took from the British economists Adam Smith and David Ricardo the idea that labor is the source of economic value. But he pressed that claim further. If labor creates value, he asked, where does profit come from? His answer was surplus value: workers produce more than they are paid, and the difference is taken by those who own the means of production. This was not, in his view, a matter of individual greed. It was a built-in feature of how capitalist production works.

He paired this economic argument with a theory of history. The driving force of historical change, he argued, is conflict between groups defined by their economic position. Those who control productive resources and those who supply labor have fundamentally opposed interests. That conflict eventually breaks existing arrangements apart and creates new ones. This framework is called historical materialism: the claim that economic conditions shape political and social institutions, not the reverse. It was a direct challenge to the common view that ideas, laws, and culture were what moved history forward.
general.centralIdea:
Capital, published in 1867, opens with a close analysis of the commodity, which Marx treats as the basic unit of capitalist production. Under capitalism, he argues, social relations between people come to appear as relations between things. A coat has a price. The specific human labor behind it disappears from view. Marx called this commodity fetishism. The term is deliberate. He borrowed it from writing about societies that attributed magical properties to objects, and his point was that capitalism performs a similar trick. What is actually a relationship built on unequal exchange looks like a natural property of goods.

The broader argument in Capital is that capitalism is not simply unjust but prone to breakdown. Competitive pressure forces owners to substitute machinery for labor. Since Marx located the source of profit in labor, this shift tends over time to reduce the rate of profit across the whole economy. Periodic crises follow from this pressure. This was his most specific prediction about capitalism's long-run behavior. Historians and economists have debated whether the evidence supports it, and the answer is mixed.
general.howSpread:
After Marx's death, Engels made his work more accessible. The German Social Democratic Party adopted a broadly Marxist platform, and by the early 20th century Marxism had become the standard framework for the European labor movement.

The decisive change came with Lenin, who adapted Marx's ideas to the conditions of early-20th-century Russia. Lenin argued that a disciplined revolutionary party had to lead a working class that could not generate revolution on its own. This was a real departure from what Marx had written. Marx's own texts said little about how a revolution should be organized. The Bolshevik Revolution of 1917 brought Lenin's version of the framework to power. From there, communist parties modeled on that version spread to China, Cuba, Vietnam, and elsewhere.

One view holds that the outcomes of these revolutions owed as much to each country's specific conditions as to Marx's analysis. That argument is contested. But the distance between what Marx wrote and what these states actually built was real and significant.
general.contestedInheritance:
Marx's legacy divides along several lines. One concerns the theory of value at the core of Capital. Mainstream economics moved away from the labor theory of value in the late 19th century. Critics argue that Capital's analysis rests on a theory that does not hold up. Defenders respond that Marx's core claim, that workers are paid less than the value they produce, can be restated without the specific price theory he used.

A second dispute concerns the communist states of the 20th century. The Soviet Union, Maoist China, and others claimed Marxist legitimacy while producing authoritarian rule and, in some cases, mass violence directed at the very classes Marx said would be liberated. Whether this represents a betrayal of his ideas or a consequence that his framework helped produce is a question historians and political theorists have not resolved. Marx's own writings placed weight on workers' self-organization and democratic control. The party-state model that emerged discarded both.

His diagnosis of capitalism's pull toward inequality and its tendency toward financial crisis has drawn fresh interest since 2008, when financial markets came close to collapse and long-standing critiques of market economies suddenly seemed less distant from the news. Sales of Capital rose noticeably in several countries in the years that followed.

advanced.theFigure: The 'crisis of Marxism' — the collapse of actually existing socialism after 1989, the apparent refutation of Marx's predictions about capitalism's trajectory, and the intellectual discrediting of the communist project — generated substantial rethinking across the Marxist tradition. Some scholars retreated to cultural analysis (the legacy of the cultural turn in the 1980s–90s). Others developed post-Marxist frameworks (Laclau and Mouffe's Hegemony and Socialist Strategy). Others doubled down on rigorous economic analysis. The result has been a diversified Marxism that is more theoretically sophisticated but less politically unified than its 20th-century predecessors.
advanced.worldOfIdeas: The debate about the labor theory of value — whether it is defensible as an economic theory or whether it should be replaced by alternative frameworks for analyzing exploitation — has been one of the most technically demanding in Marxist scholarship. The 'Transformation Problem' (how values expressed in labor time are transformed into prices of production) was identified as a logical inconsistency by Böhm-Bawerk and has generated substantial attempts at resolution, including Sraffa's Production of Commodities by Means of Commodities (1960), the Temporal Single System Interpretation (TSSI), and various neo-Ricardian approaches. Whether the value theory is essential to Marx's analysis or can be replaced without loss is contested.
advanced.centralIdea: Economists have tested the tendency of the rate of profit to fall using national accounts data from major capitalist economies. The numbers show a downward trend over long periods, but with large swings and partial recoveries that make the pattern hard to read as a simple law. Robert Brenner's The Economics of Global Turbulence (2006) uses a modified version of this tendency to explain why the global economy has grown more slowly since the 1970s. Marx himself acknowledged that the tendency operates against offsetting forces, so the debate is really about whether those counterweights are temporary or permanent — and that question remains open.
advanced.howSpread: The post-colonial Marxist tradition — represented by figures including Frantz Fanon, CLR James, Walter Rodney, and more recently Cedric Robinson — has substantially developed Marxist analysis by centering race and colonial extraction rather than treating them as secondary to class. Robinson's Black Marxism (1983) argues that racism was not an ideology produced by capitalism to divide the working class but was constitutive of European civilization before capitalism — that 'racial capitalism' is a redundancy because capitalism has always been racial. This analysis has been increasingly influential in the contemporary left.
advanced.contestedInheritance: The question of Marx's relevance after the collapse of actually existing socialism has been answered differently across disciplines. In academic economics, Marxist analysis remains marginal — the mainstream rejected the labor theory of value and treats Marx as a historical figure rather than a living theorist. In sociology, cultural studies, and political theory, Marxist concepts (class, ideology, hegemony, alienation, commodity fetishism) remain widely used, often without explicit Marxist framing. In political practice, Marxist parties and movements persist, though none has achieved the revolutionary transformation Marx predicted.

foundation.theStory: Marx matters as the most systematic critic of capitalism, as the thinker whose analysis of class, exploitation, and historical materialism defined the terms of socialist and communist politics for 150 years, and as a historical figure whose ideas — claimed and distorted by 20th-century communist regimes — shaped events that killed tens of millions of people. Understanding him requires distinguishing what he actually wrote from what was done in his name.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern whether the labor theory of value is defensible, whether historical materialism is a coherent theory of history, whether the gap between Marx's analysis and 20th-century communist practice represents betrayal or logical development, and whether his critique of capitalism remains relevant after the collapse of the communist alternative.
foundation.whyItMatters: Marx matters for understanding capitalism, the Industrial Revolution, the Russian Revolution, Stalinism, the Cold War, inequality, class analysis, and the history of socialist and communist politics.
questions: Marx argued that the economic structure of society determines its political, legal, and cultural institutions — that 'it is not the consciousness of men that determines their existence, but their social existence that determines their consciousness.' Is this a testable claim? What evidence would support or refute it?

The communist regimes of the 20th century — Soviet Union, China, Cambodia — claimed Marxist legitimacy while producing outcomes Marx did not predict: authoritarian states, new ruling classes, mass violence against workers and peasants. Does this refute Marx's analysis, or does it demonstrate only that Marxism was distorted by specific historical conditions? How would you distinguish between these possibilities?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential primary texts are Capital Volume 1 (Harvey's Companion is the essential guide), The Communist Manifesto (with Engels), The German Ideology (for historical materialism), and the Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844 (for alienation). Stedman Jones's biography is the essential scholarly account. David Harvey's work provides the most systematic contemporary development.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: Marx's texts are extensive, technically demanding, and internally complex — Capital alone is over 900 pages in Volume 1, with two more volumes of notes. The relationship between the early Marx (1844 Manuscripts) and the mature Marx (Capital) — whether there is a fundamental break or continuous development — has generated a substantial interpretive literature. The main evidentiary limits are the gap between Marx's theoretical predictions and subsequent historical developments, which require either revising the theory or explaining why the predictions didn't obtain.
interpretation.takingAPosition:
The most defensible scholarly position on the labor theory of value is that it is not defensible as a price theory — commodity prices in practice do not track socially necessary labor time with any reliability — but that this does not make Marx's project of understanding exploitation and surplus extraction incoherent. The analytical point Marx was making — that in a capitalist system, workers produce more value than they receive, and that this surplus is appropriated by those who own the means of production — survives the technical failure of the specific price theory. Contemporary analytical Marxists (Roemer, Cohen, Wright) have reformulated the exploitation argument using tools from mainstream economics that avoid the labor theory's problems while preserving its core insight. A strong scholarly argument must distinguish between the specific mechanism Marx proposed and the underlying claim about exploitation, which can be defended on different grounds.

On the relationship between Marx's analysis and 20th-century communist regimes, the most defensible position is that the connection is neither inevitable nor irrelevant. Marx's political writings — particularly his advocacy of revolutionary vanguard organizations and his analysis of the 'dictatorship of the proletariat' as a transitional form — provided resources that Leninist parties used to justify authoritarian governance. But Marx also wrote extensively about workers' self-organization, democratic accountability, and the dangers of bureaucratic power. Which elements of Marx were selected, emphasized, and institutionalized depended on the specific political circumstances of each revolution. The connection is real but contingent.

On Marx's continuing relevance after 1989, the strongest scholarly position is that the post-1989 resurgence of interest in Marx reflects genuine analytical continuities between his framework and observable features of contemporary capitalism — rising inequality, recurrent financial crises, the commodification of domains previously outside market logic — rather than ideological nostalgia. The 2008 financial crisis in particular demonstrated the continuing relevance of Marx's analysis of financialization and the tendency toward crisis, even for scholars who do not accept his political prescriptions.
questions: Marx argued that under capitalism, workers are alienated — separated from the products of their labor, from the process of production, from other workers, and from their own human potential. This is a normative claim: it requires a conception of what non-alienated labor would look like. Is Marx's concept of alienation coherent? And if alienation is real, does it require capitalism's abolition or merely its reform?

Gramsci argued that capitalism maintains itself through cultural hegemony — producing common sense that makes existing arrangements appear natural and inevitable. If this is correct, how would a dominated class ever develop the consciousness required for revolutionary change? Is Gramsci's concept of hegemony compatible with Marx's historical materialism, or does it undermine it?

research.topic: The intellectual relationship between Marx and Hegel
research.content: The relationship between Marx and Hegel — the nature of Marx's 'materialist inversion,' how much of Hegel's dialectical method Marx retained, and the significance of his early Hegelian writings for understanding Capital — has been extensively analyzed. The 'rupture' thesis (Althusser) argued for a sharp break between the young humanist Marx and the mature scientific Marx. The 'continuity' thesis (Lukács, Ollman) argued that the early and late works form a coherent whole. Recent scholarship (Arthur's The New Dialectic and Marx's Capital) has reconstructed the systematic relationship between Hegel's Logic and Capital's structure.

research.topic: The relationship between Marx's value theory and contemporary capitalism
research.content: The application of Marxist value theory to contemporary capitalism — particularly financialization, platform capitalism, and the 'general intellect' (immaterial labor, cognitive capitalism) — has generated substantial theoretical development. Autonomist Marxists (Negri, Hardt) argue that contemporary capitalism has superseded the categories of Capital because value is increasingly produced by social cooperation and knowledge that cannot be measured in labor time. Traditional Marxists argue that value theory remains applicable. The empirical testing of these claims using national accounts data is an active research area.

What evidence would resolve this: a complete manuscript of Capital Volumes 2 and 3 in Marx's own final form, rather than the reconstructions from notebooks that Engels edited after Marx's death. Why unavailable: Marx died before completing Capital; Volumes 2 and 3 are Engels's reconstructions from manuscript fragments in varying states of completion; the relationship between what Engels published and what Marx would have written is genuinely uncertain. What can be established: the manuscript materials that Engels worked from, now substantially available through the MEGA2 (Marx-Engels Gesamtausgabe) edition. What cannot be established: how Marx would have resolved the theoretical tensions in the unfinished manuscripts, particularly around the transformation problem.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Revolutionary Science
comparativeNarrative.content: Leninists see Marx as the founder of a science of revolution. His theory of history, they argue, gives a small, disciplined group of revolutionaries leading on the workers' behalf the tools to carry out socialist revolution, even in a country with few factory workers. On this view, the communist states of the 20th century were not betrayals of Marx. They were attempts to build socialism under pressure: poor countries facing military threats and trade blockades from richer powers. When those states failed or turned harsh, Leninists point to outside forces, not to Marx's ideas. Marxism-Leninism and Maoism, in their view, are honest updates of Marx to fit local conditions.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Critical Theory
comparativeNarrative.content: A different strand of Marxism drew a hard line between Marx and what Lenin built. Marx wanted workers to run their own lives. What appeared in the Soviet Union was a party-state that ran things for them. This group of thinkers, which included Gramsci and the Frankfurt School, argued that 20th-century communist states had swapped genuine worker control for rule by party officials, which was the kind of top-down power Marx had criticized. They also shifted focus from armed revolt to culture. Their key question was: how does capitalism get people to accept a world that does not serve them? They argued that Marx's thinking still helps answer that question.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Brilliant Mistake
comparativeNarrative.content: Liberal critics accept that Marx was a serious thinker, but they argue that his key claims have been proved wrong. Workers in rich countries did not grow steadily poorer. They grew richer. The idea that labor alone creates economic value has not stood up to how prices actually work in markets. And the states that ruled in Marx's name, the Soviet Union, Maoist China, Cambodia under the Khmer Rouge, killed millions of people. Liberal critics say this was not a string of bad luck. Marx's writings gave later leaders a way to justify holding on to power. Still, some of his ideas, about how class shapes politics, about how people absorb the values of those who rule them, remain worth taking seriously on their own terms.

rabbitHole.label: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel
rabbitHole.reason: Marx's entire method — the dialectical analysis of capitalism's contradictions, historical materialism, the concept of alienation — derives from a materialist transformation of Hegel's idealist dialectic.

rabbitHole.label: Capitalism
rabbitHole.reason: Capital is the most systematic analysis of how capitalism works — Marx's concepts of surplus value, commodity fetishism, and the tendency toward crisis are the foundational critique of market economies.

rabbitHole.label: The Communist Manifesto
rabbitHole.reason: The Manifesto — written with Engels in 1848 — is the most widely read political document in history and the essential short statement of Marx's analysis of capitalism and his prediction of revolutionary transformation.

rabbitHole.label: The Russian Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The Bolshevik Revolution was the first revolution to claim Marxist legitimacy — Lenin's adaptation of Marx's analysis to Russian conditions produced a political form (the vanguard party, the one-party state) that Marx's texts do not clearly endorse.

rabbitHole.label: The Soviet Union under Stalin
rabbitHole.reason: Stalinist Soviet Union claimed to be constructing socialism on Marxist foundations — the gap between Marx's analysis and Stalinist practice is the most consequential and contested question in the history of Marxism.

rabbitHole.label: Inequality
rabbitHole.reason: Marx's analysis of how capitalism structurally produces inequality — through surplus value extraction, the concentration of capital, and the reserve army of labor — remains the foundational theoretical account of economic inequality.

rabbitHole.label: The Industrial Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The Industrial Revolution was the historical context for Marx's analysis — the specific form of capitalism he examined was the factory-based industrial capitalism of 19th-century England, which he observed directly in Manchester and analyzed through the British Museum's statistical records.

rabbitHole.label: John Maynard Keynes
rabbitHole.reason: Keynes and Marx represent the two great critiques of laissez-faire capitalism — Keynes arguing for managed capitalism through fiscal policy, Marx arguing for capitalism's revolutionary transcendence. Their relationship to each other defines the political economy of the 20th century.

reference.author: Marx, Karl
reference.title: Capital: A Critique of Political Economy, Volume 1
reference.annotation: The essential primary text — the most systematic analysis of how capitalism works. Harvey's Companion is the indispensable guide. The Penguin Classics edition (translated by Ben Fowkes) is the standard English text.

reference.author: Stedman Jones, Gareth
reference.title: Karl Marx: Greatness and Illusion
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly biography — situates Marx in his intellectual context and provides a more historically grounded account than either hagiographic or polemical treatments.

reference.author: Harvey, David
reference.title: A Companion to Marx's Capital
reference.annotation: The essential guide to reading Capital — Harvey's close reading makes the argument accessible without simplifying it. The necessary companion to the primary text.

reference.author: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels
reference.title: The Communist Manifesto
reference.annotation: Named among the essential primary texts — the 1848 work, co-authored with Engels, that the entry treats as a primary source for Marx's political program.

reference.author: Cedric Robinson
reference.title: Black Marxism: The Making of the Black Radical Tradition
reference.annotation: The source of the 'racial capitalism' argument the Advanced layer engages — that racism preceded and was constitutive of European capitalism rather than a byproduct of it.

reference.author: Marx, Karl
reference.title: The German Ideology
reference.annotation: The primary text the Interpretation layer names as the source for Marx's historical materialism, distinct from the economic argument of Capital.

reference.author: Marx, Karl
reference.title: Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844
reference.annotation: The primary text the entry treats as the source for Marx's concept of alienation and the 'early Marx,' central to the continuity-versus-rupture debate the Interpretation layer engages.

reference.author: Brenner, Robert
reference.title: The Economics of Global Turbulence
reference.annotation: The Advanced layer engages Brenner's thesis directly — that a modified tendency of the rate of profit to fall explains the global economy's slower growth since the 1970s.

reference.author: Laclau, Ernesto and Mouffe, Chantal
reference.title: Hegemony and Socialist Strategy
reference.annotation: Named in the Advanced layer as the exemplary post-Marxist framework developed in response to the 'crisis of Marxism' after 1989.

reference.author: Sraffa, Piero
reference.title: Production of Commodities by Means of Commodities
reference.annotation: Cited in the Advanced layer as one of the principal attempts to resolve the Transformation Problem in the labor-theory-of-value debate.

reference.author: Arthur, Christopher J.
reference.title: The New Dialectic and Marx's Capital
reference.annotation: Named in the research layer as the recent reconstruction of the systematic relationship between Hegel's Logic and the structure of Capital.

commerce.title: The Communist Manifesto
commerce.author: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels
commerce.note: Start here — at 40 pages, the most widely read political document in history is the right entry point; its diagnosis of capitalism's dynamism and its prediction of its collapse are still worth engaging on their merits, whatever you conclude.

commerce.title: Karl Marx: Greatness and Illusion
commerce.author: Gareth Stedman Jones
commerce.note: The essential scholarly biography — Stedman Jones situates Marx in his intellectual context and challenges standard readings; the most historically rigorous account of what Marx actually argued versus what has been attributed to him.

commerce.title: A Companion to Marx's Capital
commerce.author: David Harvey
commerce.note: The essential guide to reading Capital — Harvey's close reading makes the argument of Marx's masterwork accessible without simplifying it; the necessary companion to the primary text for any serious reader.

commerce.title: Capital: A Critique of Political Economy, Vol. 1
commerce.author: Karl Marx (trans. Ben Fowkes)
commerce.note: The essential primary source — the most systematic analysis of how capitalism works ever written; the Fowkes Penguin translation is the standard. Read chapters 1–3 and 25–33 first; return to the middle with Harvey's guide.

commerce.title: Black Marxism: The Making of the Black Radical Tradition
commerce.author: Cedric Robinson
commerce.note: The essential extension and critique — Robinson's argument that capitalism has always been racial capitalism, and that the Black radical tradition represents an alternative to both Marxism and liberalism, is the most important recent development in the Marxist tradition.

popularCulture.title: Alienation
popularCulture.description: When people say they feel 'alienated' at work, they borrow a word Marx made famous. He meant something exact: workers are cut off from what they make, from how they make it, and from each other. The word now floats through psychology, philosophy, and daily speech. Most people who reach for it have no idea Marx coined this use.

popularCulture.title: The specter of communism
popularCulture.description: The Communist Manifesto opens with a line: 'A specter is haunting Europe — the specter of communism.' In 1917 the specter became a state. By 1991 many called Marx finished. Then the 2008 crash and the widening wealth gap of the 2010s pulled readers back to him. The phrase keeps returning because the troubles he named keep returning with it.

popularCulture.title: The Young Karl Marx — Raoul Peck (2017)
popularCulture.description: The film follows Marx and Engels through the 1840s, showing Marx as a prickly, driven man tangled in his own contradictions rather than a marble bust. The ideas themselves resist the screen. Long arguments about value and class shrink into a few quick scenes, so a viewer leaves with the man clearly drawn and the thinking barely sketched.

popularCulture.title: 20th-century communism and the question of responsibility
popularCulture.description: The Soviet Union, Maoist China, and the Khmer Rouge all claimed Marx as their guide, and all carried out mass killing. People split hard over his share of the blame. Some say his ideas were bent by the men who seized power. Others say his writings handed those men ready excuses. Your answer colors how you read everything else he wrote.

popularCulture.title: Marx after 2008
popularCulture.description: When markets nearly collapsed in 2008, sales of Capital climbed in several countries. Marx had said that crashes are stitched into capitalism itself, and that claim looked sharper afterward. Thomas Piketty's Capital in the Twenty-First Century (2013) piled up data showing wealth pooling at the top, the same drift Marx had traced more than a century before.

comparativeSummary: Almost no one reads Marx without already knowing what was done in his name. That is the problem these cards circle. The split is not really about economics; it is about who owns the wreckage of the 20th century. Leninists claim the revolutions as Marx applied under hard conditions. Western Marxists say those party-states betrayed his demand that workers run their own lives. Liberals say his core predictions simply failed, and that his writings handed later rulers an excuse. Same man, three verdicts, because each side keeps a different distance between the books and the regimes.
